items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.correct: Shareholders' personal assets are protected if the company fails — they can only lose the amount they invested, not their personal wealth.
items.misconception: Limited liability means the company has limited debts it can take on.
items.random_wrong: Limited liability means a business can only operate for a limited time.
items.why: Limited liability is about the LIMIT on what shareholders can lose, not on what the company can do. Students misread the term as restricting the business rather than protecting investors.

items.q: When might an autocratic leadership style be appropriate?
items.correct: In crises requiring quick decisions, when workers are inexperienced, or in highly hierarchical contexts — though it can damage morale long-term.
items.misconception: Autocratic leadership is always wrong and democratic leadership is always right.
items.random_wrong: Autocratic leaders are dictators who break the law.
items.why: Students moralise leadership styles. The business studies view is contingency-based — each style has appropriate contexts. Democratic is not universally best; autocracy works in emergencies.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SMART objectives stand for?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Specific. “Strategic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which 1970 essay argued the 'social responsibility of business is to increase profits'?
items.why: It's Friedman. “Drucker” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many elements are in the SMART acronym?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 elements. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
